summary: Graham argues that as organizations grow, they pile on protective checks without accounting for their hidden costs, which can outweigh the intended benefits. These costs are especially damaging in software, where delays in shipping kill productivity and morale. The core claim: excessive checks repel top talent and make companies less effective.
key_points: Checks are added after mistakes but rarely weighed against their hidden opportunity costs
Approval processes can distort markets and make companies pay more for worse suppliers
Government and corporate checks can create discontinuous, economy-wide harms
Programmers thrive on shipping; release checks drastically reduce productivity and satisfaction
Too many checks deter great programmers from working for you at all
money_quotes: The purpose of the committee is presumably to ensure that the company doesn't waste money. And yet the result is that the company pays 10 times as much.
The only people who will sell to you are companies that specialize in selling to you. Then you've sunk to a whole new level of inefficiency.
They'd have sacrificed hundreds of thousands of dollars, perhaps millions, just to be able to deliver more software to users.
So if you don't let people ship, you won't have any artists.
key_insight: Unchecked accumulation of organizational safeguards backfires by raising hidden costs and driving away the very talent that makes progress possible.
